title: Unicode and SignWriting Overview
subtitle: Unicode history, compatibility, and standards-facing discussion
summary: Unicode history, current production encodings, compatibility limits, developer guidance, Wikimedia context, and standards-facing next steps belong in one technical record.

slides.title: Unicode Record And Compatibility
slides.summary: Unicode history, compatibility issues, current production encodings, and standards-facing SignWriting work need to be read together.

image_prompts.concrete: Technical standards overview slide showing Unicode, FSW, SWU, compatibility, developer notes, and UTC-facing documents
image_prompts.abstract: Standards records and encoding layers aligned around SignWriting

bullets: Compatibility between Unicode, FSW, and SWU
slides.speech: Unicode history and current production practice need to be read together. The record includes Unicode proposals and approvals, but the compatibility question depends on whether official Unicode SignWriting can carry the actual Sutton SignWriting production ecosystem.

slides.title: Central Distinction
slides.summary: Official Unicode encoding and the production text models that currently make SignWriting usable are related but not identical.

image_prompts.concrete: Comparison slide showing official Unicode SignWriting on one side and FSW/SWU production encodings on the other, with compatibility questions between them
image_prompts.abstract: Two encoding paths connected by compatibility bridges

bullets: Unicode approval is part of the record
Production use still depends on FSW and SWU
slides.speech: The central distinction is that official Unicode history and current production encodings are related but not identical. Unicode approval, FSW, SWU, compatibility, rendering, and standards work each need to be kept in their proper places.
slides.notes: Keep the distinction precise and non-defensive.

slides.title: Reading Path
slides.summary: Readers can move from the overview into chronology, Unicode overview, UTC technical note, developer notes, and Wikimedia context.

image_prompts.concrete: Unicode series reading path slide moving from overview to chronology, Unicode overview, UTC technical note, developer notes, and Wikimedia brief
image_prompts.abstract: A standards reading path through chronology, compatibility, and implementation

slides.speech: The clearest path starts with the overview and chronology. Then readers can move into the Unicode overview, UTC technical note, developer notes, and Wikimedia brief depending on whether they need history, standards analysis, implementation guidance, or public-platform context.

slides.title: Scope Boundary
slides.summary: The series critiques compatibility limits in the current official SignWriting model; it does not argue against Unicode or standards work.

image_prompts.concrete: Standards boundary slide separating Unicode as an important standards system from specific SignWriting compatibility gaps in symbol identity, written signs, sorting, and migration
image_prompts.abstract: A precise technical critique held inside a standards-facing boundary

bullets: Unicode remains an important standards context
Future work should start from accurate distinctions
slides.speech: Unicode remains an important standards context. The critique here is compatibility-specific: the current official SignWriting model has limits around symbol identity, written signs, sorting, tooling, and migration. Future work should start from those distinctions.
slides.notes: Use this to keep the series technical rather than oppositional.
